scope: How to evaluate embeddings and the risk of domain shift when transfer fails.
key_concepts: There is no universally perfect embedding; quality is strictly defined by how well the representation separates classes or captures similarities for a specific downstream task.
A common evaluation method is linear probing: training a simple linear classifier on frozen embeddings. High accuracy indicates the embeddings successfully map the desired patterns.
Domain shift is the primary reason transferred representations fail. It happens when the target application's data distribution is fundamentally different from the pretraining data.
Under severe domain shift, frozen extractors generate overlapping, uninformative vectors that lack predictive utility.
When frozen transfer fails, the pipeline must shift along the adaptation spectrum towards fine-tuning or domain-specific pretraining.

definitions.definition: The divergence between the data distribution a model was pretrained on and the data it encounters in the target task.
Evaluating embedding quality by training a simple linear model on top of frozen feature vectors.

examples: A vision model pretrained on everyday objects (like ImageNet) extracting poor embeddings for X-ray scans, as the medical anomalies look nothing like natural images.

relations.precedes: ch15_s04 (the boundary between transferring external representations and compressing internal data).

exclusions: Do not detail complex domain adaptation algorithms or adversarial domain training.
